config.heroHeadline: I want calmer stress response. What does the evidence actually support?
config.orientationHeading: Start by naming the stress pattern
config.orientationSubtext: The same ingredient can look useful or weak depending on acute pressure, baseline tension, reactivity, and sleep spillover.
config.safetyHeading: Stress-support decisions change when risk context changes
config.safetyBody: Do not use supplements to mask persistent panic symptoms, severe mood instability, unsafe medication interactions, or other complex clinical situations.

acute_stress.title: Acute stress
acute_stress.description: Short-term spikes in tension, pressure, or overwhelm.

baseline_tension.title: Baseline tension
baseline_tension.description: Persistent background strain that does not fully reset.

cognitive_stress_load.title: Cognitive stress load
cognitive_stress_load.description: Rumination, cognitive fatigue, or pressure-linked focus disruption.

stress_reactivity.title: Stress reactivity
stress_reactivity.description: Feeling easily triggered with stronger physical or emotional responses.

stress_sleep_spillover.title: Stress-sleep spillover
stress_sleep_spillover.description: Stress carrying into bedtime, sleep continuity, or next-day recovery.

claims.claim_statement: L-theanine is a plausible first-pass option when the stress pattern is acute tension or task-linked physiological arousal.
claims.evidence_summary: Small human stress-challenge studies report changes in heart-rate, immune, or subjective stress responses after L-theanine exposure.
claims.limitations: The evidence base is small, stress tasks are artificial, and effects may be subtle in high-pressure real-world settings.
claims.best_fit: Presentation nerves, acute work pressure, or stimulation smoothing where sedation is not desired.
claims.not_best_fit: Panic-level symptoms, unsafe medication combinations, or situations needing immediate clinical support.

claims.claim_statement: Ashwagandha has the clearest stress fit when the issue is ongoing perceived stress rather than a single acute spike.
claims.evidence_summary: Randomized studies in stressed adults report improvements in perceived stress or anxiety-adjacent scales, with some cortisol-related signals.
claims.limitations: Trials vary by extract, dose, population, and sponsorship; safety context matters more than broad adaptogen marketing suggests.
claims.best_fit: Persistent daily strain where a delayed-onset adaptogen experiment is acceptable.
claims.not_best_fit: Pregnancy, breastfeeding, liver concerns, autoimmune or thyroid complexity, or bipolar-spectrum risk without clinician guidance.

claims.claim_statement: Rhodiola is most decision-useful when stress shows up as fatigue, pressure-linked cognitive load, or reduced mental stamina.
claims.evidence_summary: Human trials in stress-related fatigue suggest possible fatigue and attention benefits, but broader reviews describe mixed and limited evidence.
claims.limitations: Effects may depend on extract standardization and may feel activating for sensitive users.
claims.best_fit: Demanding work periods where fatigue and cognitive strain dominate over sedation needs.
claims.not_best_fit: Overstimulation, insomnia-prone patterns, bipolar-spectrum risk, or stimulant-sensitive stress states.

claims.claim_statement: Phosphatidylserine is a niche option for stress-reactivity questions involving cortisol or ACTH response patterns.
claims.evidence_summary: Small human stress-challenge studies using phosphatidic-acid and phosphatidylserine complexes report endocrine and psychological response signals.
claims.limitations: Evidence is narrow, often formula-specific, and does not establish broad everyday stress usefulness.
claims.best_fit: Research-oriented users comparing stress-reactivity options after lower-risk basics are addressed.
claims.not_best_fit: Medication complexity, pregnancy or breastfeeding, or expectations of obvious acute calming.

claims.claim_statement: Magnesium is most decision-useful when stress overlaps with low intake context, muscle tension, or low-magnesium risk signals.
claims.evidence_summary: Human reviews and trials suggest possible subjective stress or anxiety benefits, especially in low-magnesium or combined-nutrient contexts.
claims.limitations: Benefits are not uniform, many studies use combinations, and form, dose, kidney status, and gastrointestinal tolerance matter.
claims.best_fit: A conservative routine where low dietary intake or physical tension may be relevant.
claims.not_best_fit: Kidney disease, severe symptoms, or expectations of a direct sedative effect.

claims.claim_statement: L-theanine may be a gentle fit when stress carries into sleep quality or bedtime cognitive tension.
claims.evidence_summary: Human research is stronger for relaxation and stress-related symptoms than for insomnia, but some trials include sleep-quality signals.
claims.limitations: Sleep-specific effects may be modest and should not distract from persistent insomnia, apnea signs, or medication review.
claims.best_fit: Evening rumination or stress-related wind-down problems where next-day heaviness is a concern.
claims.not_best_fit: Frequent night waking, suspected sleep disorders, sedative stacking, or alcohol-based wind-down routines.

safetyNotes.warning: Autoimmune and thyroid contexts can change the safety calculus for ashwagandha.
safetyNotes.decision_effect: Pause self-directed use when autoimmune disease, thyroid medication, or thyroid instability is relevant.

safetyNotes.warning: Rare liver-injury reports make liver disease, abnormal liver labs, or jaundice symptoms important stop-and-review contexts.
safetyNotes.decision_effect: Avoid self-directed use when liver risk context is present or symptoms appear.

safetyNotes.warning: Pregnancy and breastfeeding are poor contexts for self-directed ashwagandha use.
safetyNotes.decision_effect: Do not use ashwagandha as a self-directed stress experiment in pregnancy or breastfeeding.

safetyNotes.warning: People prone to low blood pressure or lightheadedness should be cautious with calming stacks.
safetyNotes.decision_effect: Start conservatively and avoid combining with other blood-pressure-lowering inputs without review.

safetyNotes.warning: Stacking L-theanine with sedatives, alcohol, or multiple calming agents can make effects less predictable.
safetyNotes.decision_effect: Avoid stacking until the single-ingredient response and medication context are clear.

safetyNotes.warning: Kidney disease changes magnesium handling and can make supplementation unsafe without clinician guidance.
safetyNotes.decision_effect: Do not use magnesium as a self-directed stress experiment with kidney disease.

safetyNotes.warning: Medication complexity, anticoagulant use, or neurological medication context should be reviewed before phosphatidylserine experimentation.
safetyNotes.decision_effect: Avoid using phosphatidylserine as a casual stress-reactivity experiment when medication risk is unclear.

safetyNotes.warning: Rhodiola can feel activating and may be a poor fit for bipolar-spectrum risk, agitation, or insomnia-prone stress.
safetyNotes.decision_effect: Prefer non-activating options or clinician guidance when activation risk matters.

l-theanine-acute-stress.title: L-Theanine reduces psychological and physiological stress responses
l-theanine-acute-stress.source_note: Small human acute stress task study; useful for acute stress physiology context.

ashwagandha-baseline-tension.title: An investigation into the stress-relieving and pharmacological actions of an ashwagandha extract
ashwagandha-baseline-tension.source_note: Randomized trial in stressed healthy adults with stress scale and hormone endpoints.

rhodiola-cognitive-stress-load.title: Standardised Rhodiola rosea SHR-5 in subjects with stress-related fatigue
rhodiola-cognitive-stress-load.source_note: Human trial in stress-related fatigue; supports fatigue and cognitive-load framing.

phosphatidylserine-stress-reactivity.title: Soy lecithin phosphatidic acid and phosphatidylserine complex responses to mental stress
phosphatidylserine-stress-reactivity.source_note: Human mental stressor study focused on endocrine and psychological stress responses.

magnesium-baseline-tension.title: The effects of magnesium supplementation on subjective anxiety and stress
magnesium-baseline-tension.source_note: Systematic review of subjective anxiety and stress evidence; highlights limitations and heterogeneity.

l-theanine-stress-sleep-spillover.title: L-theanine, stress-related symptoms, cognitive functions, and sleep-quality signals
l-theanine-stress-sleep-spillover.source_note: Human trial with stress-related symptom and sleep-quality measures; not a dedicated insomnia trial.
